title: Trump’s Anthropic spat has made the case for AI sovereignty
gist: Ethan Zuckerman argues the Trump administration's designation of Anthropic as a 'supply chain risk' and its export bans have made the case for national 'AI sovereignty'—which, given China's closed US-China performance gap and open models, is likely to be built on Chinese code rather than US systems.

insights.claim: Anthropic refused defence secretary Pete Hegseth's demand for 'any lawful use' of its tools by the Department of Defense, with CEO Dario Amodei objecting to use for 'mass domestic surveillance' or 'fully autonomous weapons'.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: A rare case of an AI firm's ethics stance triggering direct US government retaliation.

insights.claim: Hegseth declared Anthropic a 'supply chain risk'—the first US company to receive a designation usually reserved for Chinese telecoms firms like Huawei and ZTE—and Trump instructed the government to stop using its tools.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Signals politicised, weaponised procurement designations now reaching US-domiciled AI firms.

insights.claim: Standing up to the US government likely earned Anthropic new customers, market share taken from OpenAI in 2026, and helped it recruit and retain top AI talent.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Contrarian read that principled refusal became a competitive advantage in the AI market.
insights.locator: But standing up to the US government

insights.claim: Virginia Tech scholar Lee Vinsel's concept of 'criti-hype' describes how accepting AI companies' world-disrupting claims at face value and then critiquing them ends up legitimating those same outsized claims.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: A lens for discounting AI-capability claims that double as marketing.

insights.claim: Anthropic restricted its most powerful model, Mythos 5, to 'Project Glasswing'—a select group including Google, Microsoft, Amazon Web Services and the Linux Foundation—while releasing only the safer Fable 5 for general use.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Tiered model access to critical-infrastructure incumbents illustrates emerging AI-capability gatekeeping.

insights.claim: AI safety warnings from leaders like Sam Altman are both sincere and a form of PR conveying 'our tools are so powerful, they have to come with warning labels'.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Encourages skepticism toward safety-warning theatrics as a demand-generation tactic.

insights.claim: On 12 June the US Commerce Department ordered Anthropic to suspend Mythos and Fable access to all foreign nationals, including its own employees, then dropped the restrictions less than three weeks later.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the US will use trade restrictions to control access to the most powerful AI systems.

insights.claim: The US floated then scrapped an executive order requiring 90 days to review new AI models pre-release, replacing it with a request that firms 'voluntarily' give 30 days to a new 'cybersecurity clearinghouse'.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: An evolving US pre-release review regime that adds regulatory friction to AI model launches.

insights.claim: The Trump administration effectively made the case for 'AI sovereignty', prompting technology advisers to urge governments from Ireland to Israel to develop their own AI tools lest the US cut off access.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: A structural tailwind for national and non-US AI infrastructure investment.
insights.locator: The Anthropic ban also came at an opportune time

themes.theme: Closed US-China gap and cloud concentration

insights.claim: Stanford's 2026 AI Index states 'The US-China AI model performance gap has effectively closed', noting DeepSeek briefly matched top US models in 2025 and Anthropic's best model is now only a few percentage points ahead of strong Chinese models.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: The narrowed capability gap undermines any assumption of durable US AI leadership.

insights.claim: Because many Chinese AI models are 'open' and downloadable to run on local hardware, the author predicts that if the future is AI sovereignty it is likely to be built on Chinese code, not US systems.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Open Chinese models could become the default substrate for sovereign AI—a strategic loss for US vendors.
insights.locator: If companies and countries no longer want to rely

insights.claim: A Canadian Anti-Monopoly Project report found 85 per cent of Canada's cloud resources are controlled by Amazon, Microsoft and Alphabet, with the figure close to 70 per cent in most nations.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Extreme US cloud concentration is fuelling broader IT-sovereignty pushes beyond AI.
insights.locator: A report by the Canadian Anti-Monopoly Project

insights.claim: The author predicts sovereign AIs in countries like Canada and France may be built around carefully chosen national texts and archives embodying national values, versus today's models scraped haphazardly from the internet.
insights.speaker: Ethan Zuckerman
insights.so_what: Points to a differentiated, values-based model of AI development as an investable divergence.
